fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This is your anchor fund — it's a mix of stocks (about 75%) and safer options like bonds (about 25%), all in one. It's perfect for you because it smooths out the ups and downs that come with pure stock investing. It's been around since 2000, managed by one of India's largest fund houses, has a solid track record (18-19% yearly returns over 5 years), and because it's 'equity-taxed', you won't pay tax on gains until you sell — and long-term gains above ₹1.25L per year are taxed at just 12.5%. Great for your 6-year marriage goal as well as long-term wealth.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund invests in both big stable companies (Large Cap) and fast-growing medium-sized companies (Mid Cap). It's the growth engine of your portfolio — 20% yearly returns over 5 years is excellent. ICICI Prudential is a very reputable fund house with 27 years of track record. At ₹7,883 crore AUM it's a large, stable fund. For your 30-year wealth building goal, these companies have the potential to multiply your money many times over. It's equity-taxed, so long-term gains are tax-friendly.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund focuses on medium-sized companies (mid caps) which typically grow faster than big companies. Over the last 5 years, it's returned 22% per year — among the best in its category. Nippon India is a different fund house from ICICI, so you're spreading your money across two companies, which reduces the risk of depending on a single manager. This is your 'booster' fund — at 23, you have the time to ride out any short-term drops and benefit from the higher long-term growth these companies deliver.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This is a very low-cost index fund (only 0.20% yearly fee vs 1%+ for most funds). It simply tracks the Nifty Midcap 150 — the top 150 medium-sized companies in India. No active fund manager needed, which keeps costs ultra-low. Over time, these savings in fees compound significantly — on a 30-year horizon, a 0.95% fee difference can mean lakhs more in your pocket. This is from Motilal Oswal, a third fund house, giving you good diversification across three fund houses.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This is for your emergency fund goal. Your ₹50,000 existing savings should be split: ₹17,500 goes into the hybrid fund above (long-term), and ₹32,500 stays here as the start of your 3-month emergency fund. An overnight fund is like a very safe savings account — it earns ~5.5% yearly, never loses value, and you can withdraw money within 1-2 business days. Keep adding to this until you reach your 3-month salary target (likely ₹45,000–₹60,000 depending on your salary). Note: Since you're in the new tax regime with a low income, tax on this is minimal.

plan.setup_phase:
**Step 1 — Open a direct mutual fund account (takes ~15 minutes)**
Download Kuvera (kuvera.in) or Groww app. These are free platforms where you can invest in 'direct' plans — the cheaper version of mutual funds. Avoid investing through a bank or broker, as they push 'regular' plans that charge extra fees. You'll need: Aadhaar, PAN card, bank account details, and a selfie.

**Step 2 — Complete KYC (one-time)**
The app will walk you through a quick video KYC. It's free and usually done in 24 hours.

**Step 3 — Start your emergency fund first**
Transfer your ₹50,000 savings: Put ₹32,500 in the ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund (lumpsum). Put ₹17,500 in the ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund (lumpsum). This money is your safety net — don't touch it unless it's a real emergency.

**Step 4 — Set up your 4 monthly SIPs**
On the 5th of every month (after your salary credit), set these SIPs:
- ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund: ₹2,800/month
- ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund: ₹2,400/month
- Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund: ₹1,600/month
- Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund: ₹1,200/month
Total: ₹8,000/month — exactly your surplus.

**Step 5 — Build your emergency fund over time**
Every month, check your Overnight Fund balance. Once your portfolio SIPs are running, you can direct any extra money (Diwali bonus, etc.) to the Overnight Fund until it hits 3 months of your salary.

**Step 6 — Increase SIPs every year**
Set a reminder every April (start of financial year) to increase each SIP by 10%. Even small increases make a huge difference over 30 years.
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Check that all 4 SIPs are running without failures. If any SIP bounced, ensure your bank account has sufficient balance on the 5th of each month. Consider setting up auto-debit 2 days after your salary credit date.
After 6 months: Check your emergency fund (Overnight Fund) balance. Are you on track to reach 3 months of salary? If you've got a bonus or saved extra, top it up.
After 1 year (April): Step up all SIPs by 10%. E.g., ₹2,800 becomes ₹3,080, ₹2,400 becomes ₹2,640, etc. This is the single most powerful habit you can build.

After 2 years: Check if the Nippon India Mid Cap and Motilal Oswal Index funds are broadly in line with their category peers. If a fund is consistently 4-5% behind similar funds for 2+ years, consider switching.

After 4 years (2 years before marriage goal): Move a portion of the Aggressive Hybrid fund gains to the Overnight Fund or a short-term debt fund. You want the ₹5L marriage money to be in a safe place by then — don't keep it in stocks right before you need it.
Every year in January: Check your total portfolio value and see if you're on track. Compare with the projected values in this plan.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines:
Your plan is naturally balanced because the Aggressive Hybrid fund already manages the stock/bond mix internally. Here's what to watch:

**Marriage goal (₹5 Lakhs in 6 years):** From Year 4 onward, start moving money out of equity funds into the Overnight Fund every 6 months. By Year 5.5, your ₹5L target should be fully in the Overnight Fund — safe and ready to use. Don't keep marriage money in stocks in the final year.

**Once a year check:** If mid-cap funds (Nippon + Motilal) have grown so much that they now form more than 50% of your total portfolio, shift future SIP increases into the Aggressive Hybrid or Large & Mid Cap fund to rebalance. Mid caps can zoom up — but can also fall hard.

**When NOT to rebalance:** Don't sell anything just because markets have dropped 20-30%. That's normal. Stay put. The only reason to sell is if you need the money soon or a fund is genuinely underperforming for 2+ years.
plan.rationale:
Katniss, here's the thinking behind this plan — built specifically for your life situation:

**You're 23, with 30 years ahead — that's your biggest superpower.** At this age, even small monthly amounts become life-changing wealth because of compounding (your money earns returns, and those returns earn returns, and so on for 30 years). Starting now — even at ₹8,000/month — puts you miles ahead of someone starting at 35 with ₹20,000/month.

**Every rupee counts, so we've kept costs low.** We've included the Motilal Oswal Index Fund (0.20% yearly fee) to balance the slightly higher-fee active funds. Over 30 years, that fee saving adds up to a significant amount.

**Moderate risk = mostly stocks, but cushioned.** Since you're risk-moderate, we've put ~65% in pure stock funds and ~35% in a hybrid fund that already has bonds built in. This means if markets crash 30%, your portfolio might only drop 18-22% — still painful, but manageable. And it will recover.

**Three separate goals, handled separately:**
- Emergency fund → Overnight Fund (safe, liquid, no risk)
- Marriage in 6 years → Aggressive Hybrid (grows well, not too risky, can be moved to safety 2 years before the goal)
- 30-year wealth → Large & Mid Cap + Mid Cap + Index funds (high growth, long runway to recover from any dips)

**Tax is not a worry for you right now.** In the new tax regime at your income level, you're likely in the 5% or nil slab. All the equity funds are equity-taxed (favorable rates), and you only pay tax when you sell — not while your money is growing. This is a big advantage.

**What ₹8,000/month can become:** At the base return of ~13.5% yearly, stepping up SIPs by 10% annually:
- In 10 years: ~₹24–28 lakhs
- In 20 years: ~₹1.2–1.5 crores  
- In 30 years: ~₹5–7 crores

In the bull case (17.5%), this could be ₹10+ crores. Even in the bear case (9.5%), you'd have ₹2.5–3 crores — still a very comfortable retirement.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily go down. This is completely normal and has happened multiple times in Indian market history (2008, 2020). It always recovered. Don't panic and sell. Actually, your SIPs will buy more units when markets are down — which is a good thing.

Mid-cap funds (Nippon and Motilal Oswal Index) can be extra volatile. They might fall 35-40% in a bad market crash. Since this is only 35% of your portfolio, the damage is limited. And at 23, you have decades to recover.

The marriage goal in 6 years is the most vulnerable to market timing risk. If markets are in a bad phase right in the year you need the money, you might be short. That's exactly why we've told you to start moving this money to safety 2 years before the wedding.

Inflation risk: If inflation stays high (8%+ for years), your real purchasing power grows more slowly. Equity funds are actually the best protection against this over long periods — so staying invested is the right call.

Life happens: Job loss, medical emergency, family obligations. Your emergency fund is your first line of defense. Build it to 3 months salary before anything else. If you face a crisis and have to stop SIPs temporarily, that's okay — just restart as soon as you can.

Sending money home is a noble responsibility — but don't sacrifice your own financial future entirely. Even ₹5,000–8,000/month in SIPs now will make you far more capable of helping your family in the long run.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase each SIP by 10% every April. For example, your ₹8,000 total monthly SIP becomes ₹8,800 next year, ₹9,680 the year after, and so on. As your salary grows, this becomes easier. Over 30 years, this step-up habit alone can triple your final corpus compared to keeping the same SIP forever.

aps.reasoning: 1. **passive_instrument_fraction (0.2)**: Only the Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund (35% of equity allocation) is passive. The rest are active funds. 2. **turnover_score (0.3)**: SIPs are monthly, but the plan allows for annual rebalancing and tactical adjustments (e.g., shifting marriage goal funds to debt 2 years before needed). 3. **cost_emphasis_score (0.7)**: Explicitly prioritizes low-cost index funds (0.2% expense ratio) and mentions fee savings compounding. 4. **research_vs_cost_score (0.4)**: Balances diversification across fund houses and sectors but emphasizes cost over deep research. 5. **time_horizon_alignment_score (0.8)**: Long-term SIPs (30 years) with annual step-ups align with compounding. 6. **portfolio_activeness_score (0.3)**: Most equity is in active funds with potential high tracking error, though debt is passive. Metrics suggest moderate active management.

pqs.reasoning:
goal_alignment: High alignment with goals (emergency fund, marriage, long-term) via appropriate asset allocation and timelines. Minor deduction for slight overemphasis on equity for moderate risk.
diversification: Good across asset classes, sectors, and fund houses but could improve with more international exposure.
risk_return_appropriateness: Perfect match for moderate risk with balanced equity-debt mix and downside considerations.
internal_consistency: Fully coherent strategy with clear rationale supporting each element.
tax_efficiency: Optimal use of equity-taxed funds and new regime considerations, though no international diversification.
